title: Testing a continuous-variable Bell-like inequality with a hybrid-encoded system
authors: Yu Meng, Ying Wang, Clara Henke, Nikolai Bart, Arne Ludwig, Peter Lodahl, Jonas S. Neergaard-Nielsen, Ulrik L. Andersen, Leonardo Midolo, Zheng-Hao Liu
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum systems are promising candidates for quantum computing and quantum information processing. It is widely known that quadrature measurements on Gaussian continuous-variable systems can be described by a noncontextual hidden-variable model and cannot violate a Bell inequality. Here, we demonstrate that the observation fails when sequential measurements are involved. Our experiment is realized by mapping the spatial modes of a single photon, deterministically generated from an InAs/GaAs quantum emitter, to the logical operations in the Gottesman--Kitaev--Preskill code space. Employing a black-box-style approach, we observe a violation of the Bell-like noncontextual hidden-variable inequality by 380 standard deviations. Our results address the conceptual loopholes in previous works and open up new possibilities for studying fundamental quantum physics using photonic-encoded continuous-variable systems.
summary: The researchers successfully demonstrated a violation of a continuous-variable Bell-like inequality by 380 standard deviations. They achieved this by using a hybrid system that combines photon polarization (DV) with the spatial modes of a single photon (CV). By mapping spatial modes to the GKP code space and using a Hadamard test, they bypassed the limitations of Gaussian measurements. This experiment provides a robust way to study quantum contextuality in unbounded-dimension systems.
main_problem: Testing noncontextuality (Kochen-Specker contextuality) in continuous-variable (CV) quantum systems, specifically overcoming the limitation that Gaussian CV systems cannot violate Bell-like inequalities under standard quadrature measurements.
main_result: Demonstration of a violation of a CV Bell-like noncontextual hidden-variable inequality by 380 standard deviations using a hybrid-encoded system.
method: A black-box-style approach using the Hadamard test to probe expectation values of displacement operators and a quantum switch-like setup to measure the non-commutation fraction of operators.
model_or_system: A hybrid-encoded photonic system where a discrete-variable (DV) qubit is encoded in photon polarization and a continuous-variable (CV) system is encoded in the spatial modes of a single photon generated from an InAs/GaAs quantum dot.
key_observables: Real parts of phase-space displacement operators, non-commutation fraction (kappa), and second-order correlation function g2(0).
important_parameters: Displacement scale q0 = 3 mm, p-displacement beam deflection of 78.3 urad, and g2(0) = 0.83 +/- 0.05%.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the plane-wave approximation for the spatial wavefunction and addresses the impact of imperfect commutativity between observables.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup for measuring displacement operators and the optical toolbox; Table II-IV present the Peres-Mermin square operators, experimental expectation values, and commutativity test results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of CV contextuality, describes the hybrid experimental platform and encoding, details the implementation of displacement operators, presents the violation of the Bell-like inequality, verifies operator commutativity, and discusses the impact of experimental disturbance.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it demonstrates a high-significance violation of noncontextuality in a hybrid CV-DV architecture, providing a path to study fundamental quantum mechanics using spatial modes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experimental scheme. (a) Setup for measuring the real expectation of the displacement operators. The single photons are generated from the quantum dot in the cryostat and collected with an objective system. They propagate through a sequence of polarization-controlled x-and p-displacement boxes acting on the photonic spatial modes. A polarization measurement reveals the expectations of the CV operators. The figure shows the configuration for measuring the operators in the third row of the CV Peres–Mermin square (cf. Table I(a)); other rows and columns are designed similarly by replacing the displacement boxes. (b) Displacement operations. We realize q- and p-displacement operators...
Fig. 2. Commutativity test. This Hadamard test-like setup is used to measure the degree of incompatibility between two operators.
